query: javascript garbage collection and gc pauses interview question

competitors.label: MDN Memory management
competitors.selectionReason: MDN is the canonical reference for reachability, garbage collection, and why retained references keep memory alive.
theirStrengths: It is authoritative on garbage collection fundamentals and reachability.
theirGaps: It is less explicit on GC pauses, allocation churn, jank, and the concrete Chrome DevTools workflow for investigating them.

realWorldUseCases: If your app creates lots of short-lived objects or accidentally retains references, the GC has more work to do, and that shows up as jank.
actionableExamples: Performance panel: record a trace while reproducing jank.
followUpConfusion: Allocating aggressively in hot paths (scroll, animation frames).

realWorldUseCases: MDN covers the fundamentals correctly, but our page is more explicit about the production consequence engineers actually feel: jank from allocation churn and memory pressure.
actionableExamples: The canonical guide still provides strong conceptual examples, so the example criterion remains competitive.
followUpConfusion: Our page is stronger on the likely follow-up confusion: GC is automatic, but pause spikes and retention bugs still have to be debugged intentionally.

actionableExamples: Add one short trace-reading example later if we need more example distance over MDN.

competitors.winReason: MDN is the canonical GC reference, but our page is stronger for this prompt because it connects reachability to jank, DevTools workflow, and hot-path allocation behavior.

competitors.label: V8 trash talk: the Orinoco garbage collector
competitors.selectionReason: The V8 team article is a strong implementation-oriented reference for how modern JavaScript garbage collection works and why pauses happen.
theirStrengths: It is deeper on GC internals and on how the engine reduces pause time.
theirGaps: It is less focused on the frontend debugging workflow and on the practical retention bugs interviewers usually care about.

realWorldUseCases: You render a feed and create new objects on every scroll.
followUpConfusion: They are not a silver bullet: you still need to remove event listeners, bound caches, and avoid retaining large graphs in closures.
